frameworks.name: evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: pulsatile visual cues are integrated over time to drive a navigational decision

frameworks.name: multiplicative feedback-loop circuit
frameworks.one_line: sensory responses are gain-modulated by accumulated cognitive variables in a feedback loop

frameworks.name: Weber-Fechner law
frameworks.one_line: classical psychophysical law from which observed perceptual discrimination deviates

frameworks.name: sensory-cortex-as-input view
frameworks.one_line: view that sensory cortex only relays inputs to downstream accumulators

core_question: Do primary visual and neighboring posterior cortices serve only as sensory input, or do they carry cognitive variables during evidence-accumulation-based navigation?
key_assumptions: pulsatile visual cues can be temporally integrated to inform a navigational choice
single-neuron activity across V1, secondary visual, and retrosplenial cortices is comparable
cue-locked sensory responses are separable from cognitive modulations
manipulations: cortical area (V1 vs secondary visual vs retrosplenial)
pulsatile visual cue sequences supporting evidence accumulation
navigational task variables (cue timing, accumulated evidence, place/time, decision, reward)
key_findings: only a small fraction of V1 neurons showed purely sensory-like cue responses
neurons across all areas were sequentially active and encoded sensory through cognitive variables
per-cue sensory responses were amplitude-modulated by accumulated evidence and other cognitive quantities
perceptual discrimination deviated from the Weber-Fechner law
interpretations: sensory cortices play a more intricate, feedback-coupled role in evidence accumulation than passive input
a multiplicative feedback-loop circuit can account for the deviation from Weber-Fechner discrimination
